heading: The Warning Signal
info: Collapse is rarely a sudden break; it's a loss of force.
text: Most people think collapse is a sudden, unpredictable break. But in dynamical systems theory, failure is often a slow, measurable loss of restorative force. Complex systems, from forests to financial markets, don't just stop working—they lose their ability to bounce back.

heading: 79 Communities
info: Universal mathematical structure found at tipping points.
text: Researchers at the Technical University of Munich discovered a universal mathematical structure at so-called tipping points. By analyzing 79 different ecological communities, they found that every single one sent a measurable signal before collapse. This signal doesn't just warn of danger—it can even rank which species will go extinct first.

heading: Critical Slowing Down
info: As stability fades, the system 'remembers' its errors.
text: Think of a system as a ball in a valley—its basin of attraction. In a healthy state, the valley is deep, and the ball returns to center quickly. But as a tipping point nears, the valley becomes shallow. Recovery takes longer. This is Critical Slowing Down. The system loses its 'memory' of stability and starts to flicker between states.

heading: Amazon Tipping Point
text: In the Amazon, this math is becoming a reality. We are approaching a 25% deforestation threshold. Crossing it doesn't just mean fewer trees—it means the basin of attraction for the entire rainforest disappears. Because of hysteresis, once the forest flips into a savanna, simply replanting trees won't be enough to bring it back.
